critic_raw:
The fundamental problem is applying offline evaluation to a Q-learning policy. Q-learning is an online algorithm that updates its value function through environment interactions. When applied to a fixed historical log, the algorithm cannot distinguish between actions it would take and actions the historical policy took. Offline Q-learning on a static dataset produces a policy whose value is estimated by the same dataset that generated it — without importance weighting or offline policy evaluation corrections, the Q-value estimates will be systematically overoptimistic for actions that were rarely taken under the historical policy (e.g., large restocking orders on non-standard days).

A stratified random split applied to temporal inventory data is particularly damaging for Q-learning because TD learning requires sequential state-action-reward-next-state tuples. When the split is randomized, the 'next state' for a training tuple may be in the test set, and the model constructs its value estimates from disconnected trajectories. The temporal dependency structure that Q-learning requires is destroyed by the randomization.

The 'cumulative reward' secondary metric is undefined in the evaluation context. Cumulative reward during training is not the same as expected cumulative reward under the learned policy evaluated on held-out data. The design conflates training-time learning signal with out-of-sample policy value.

The baseline is 'expert-defined heuristic rules' — but no quantification of how these rules were designed or their known performance characteristics is provided. If the heuristic rules are specifically calibrated to the five years of training data, they are not a fair baseline for out-of-sample evaluation.
all_issues_raised: Offline Q-learning on a fixed historical log cannot be evaluated without importance weighting corrections — Q-value estimates will be overoptimistic for actions rarely taken under the historical policy, and the evaluation cannot distinguish policy improvement from Q-function overfitting to observed action frequencies.

A stratified random split destroys the sequential state-action-reward-next-state structure that Q-learning requires for TD updates — next states for training tuples may appear in the test set, producing disconnected trajectories that invalidate the value function estimates.

The 'cumulative reward' secondary metric conflates training-time reward accumulation with out-of-sample policy value — these are categorically different quantities and should not be reported as equivalent evidence of policy quality.

The heuristic baseline rules are not characterized for how they were calibrated — if they were designed with knowledge of the five-year data period, they are not a fair out-of-sample comparison for the RL policy.
all_issues_adjudicated: Offline Q-learning on a fixed historical log cannot be evaluated without importance weighting corrections — Q-value estimates will be overoptimistic for actions rarely taken under the historical policy, and the evaluation cannot distinguish policy improvement from Q-function overfitting to observed action frequencies.

A stratified random split destroys the sequential state-action-reward-next-state structure that Q-learning requires for TD updates — next states for training tuples may appear in the test set, producing disconnected trajectories that invalidate the value function estimates.

The 'cumulative reward' secondary metric conflates training-time reward accumulation with out-of-sample policy value — these are categorically different quantities and should not be reported as equivalent evidence of policy quality.

The heuristic baseline rules are not characterized for how they were calibrated — if they were designed with knowledge of the five-year data period, they are not a fair out-of-sample comparison for the RL policy.
